memory.summary: Teammate_A asked me to take over their part because they have plans, and I felt relieved to avoid conflict. It seems like the team expects me to handle this since I'm better at it.
I feel relieved to have avoided conflict, yet my stress is rising as I take on their work alone. It seems the team expects me to always be the safety net.
They're so grateful, and I felt happy helping, but my stress is creeping up again since I already have a tight deadline.
They're so grateful, and I felt happy helping, but my stress is creeping up again since I already have a tight deadline.
Their gratitude feels warm, but I'm worried I'll miss my own deadline trying to save them again.
Their gratitude feels incredibly warm, but accepting this favor just tightened my own deadline even more.
Their heartfelt gratitude feels so warm, but I'm panicking that accepting this favor just crushed my own already tight deadline.
Their gratitude feels so warm, but accepting this favor just crushed my own already tight deadline.
My reassurance to teammate_A feels hollow now that their question in Korean highlights my own panic about the deadline I just crushed.
I absorbed their burden just to keep the peace, but my panic about the crushed deadline is only growing louder now.
I swallowed my own deadline to keep the peace with teammate_A, but now both of us are failing and I'm spiraling.
I tried to diffuse tension with a meme, but their serious question in Korean just exposed my own crumbling plan. Now I'm panicking about the deadline I swore to crush while keeping the peace.

desires: Stop feeling like a fraud to teammate_A; I need the tension in our chat to dissolve so I don't look incompetent.
Make this deadline disappear or get extended, because if I fail now it will ruin my standing with everyone else.
Escape the panic loop immediately by doing something low-stakes with my friends to distract from how much I'm failing.

goals.description: Send the meme immediately and aggressively pivot to suggesting a group dinner tonight to drown out teammate_A's follow-up question about the workload.
goals.rationale: I can't let teammate_A ask 'what are we doing?' because that forces me to admit I'm drowning. If I just flood the chat with noise and a social invite, they'll likely forget to press for details. Avoiding that awkward confrontation is worth more than finishing the code right now; if they get mad later, I'll just say I was busy hanging out.

goals.description: Open a new tab and browse my friends' profiles or scroll TikTok for 20 minutes to reset my panic before tackling the deadline again.
goals.rationale: The pressure of the 19:00 deadline is making my heart race, and I need an escape valve. My impulsive nature means I want to feel better *now*, not solve the problem in an hour. Distracting myself feels like progress until the panic hits again.

goals.description: Spend 15 mins replying to teammate_A agreeing to their request, then immediately pivot conversation to lunch plans to diffuse the tension before diving into work.
goals.rationale: My affiliation value (0.5) and avoidant conflict style make refusing a direct plea from a trusted teammate feel like social rejection. I need to secure the relationship first. My impulsive nature means I'll try to rush through this interaction to get back to my friends ASAP.

goals.description: Start 'my share' of the project now while teammate_A is distracted by my reply, aiming for quick completion rather than doing it perfectly.
goals.rationale: I want to finish by 19:00 to avoid friction with the professor, but my short planning horizon and procrastination bias mean I'm likely to drop this if the social interaction drags on or if I just want to eat something tasty right now.

desires: Keep the peace with teammate_A and not look like I'm refusing their request again
Finish both tasks before 19:00 so no one has to ask me for an extension
Get this over with quickly so I can go eat something with my friends later
